content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 13, 2008

Richard Rios
Olsen Hagel & Fishburn LLP
555 Capital Mall, Suite 1425
Sacramento, CA 95814-4602

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-08-159

Dear Mr. Rios:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you seek general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

QUESTIONS

1. When reporting expenditures of campaign funds for gifts, meals, and travel under new Regulation 18421.7, do the additional disclosure rules apply only to single transactions of $100 or more, or do they apply to transactions that cumulatively reach $100 to a single vendor during a reporting period?

2. Do the additional disclosure rules under Regulation 18421.7 for an expenditure for travel apply to gas or transportation within a candidate's jurisdiction, a legislator's travel between his or her district and Sacramento, or travel within California by a statewide candidate?

3. Do the additional disclosure rules under Regulation 18421.7 for an expenditure for a meal apply to food provided to precinct walkers, a single expenditure of $100 or more to purchase both office supplies and food, catered food served at an official's fundraiser, or a room service order when traveling if the total charges to the hotel require itemization?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-08-159
Page No. 2


4. What is the definition of "gift" as used in Regulation 18421.7, and do the
exceptions for the term found in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or other
Commission regulations apply?

5. If an itemized expenditure for a gift is made for a large group of recipients,
may the committee use a term or phrase to describe the recipients?

CONCLUSIONS

1. When reporting expenditures for meals, gifts, or travel under Regulation
18421.7, the additional disclosure rules apply only to single payments of $100 or more,
or $500 or more if made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the candidate
or committee, unless a series of payments is for a single service or good.

2. As discussed below, an expenditure of campaign funds for airfare is always an
expenditure for "travel" subject to the additional disclosure requirements of Regulation
18421.7. However, payments for the costs of ground transportation within a certain area
of the candidate's residence or jurisdiction may not be subject to the additional disclosure
requirements of Regulation 18421.7 depending, as discussed below, on the office held or
sought by the candidate.

3. As discussed below, the determination of whether an expenditure for food and
beverages is subject to the additional disclosure rules for an expenditure for a meal under
Regulation 18421.7 is based on whether the food or beverages constitute a meal to the
recipient. Food and beverages not constituting a meal may, however, be subject to
additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7 as an expenditure for a gift unless the
recipient has provided equal or greater consideration.

4. Generally, a gift of campaign funds for purposes of Regulation 18421.7 is any
payment of campaign funds that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. We find no reason to extend
the exceptions to the term "gift" contained in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or any
of the other exceptions to gifts set forth in Commission regulations, to the disclosure of
gifts of campaign funds as required by Regulation 18421.7.

5. A committee may use a term or phrase to describe the recipients of a gift under
Regulation 18421.7 only when the benefit to each recipient is less than $50 or only if the
name of each recipient who received a benefit of $50 or more is also disclosed.

FACTS

Under new Regulation 18421.7, state and local candidates and their controlled
committees must provide detailed descriptions of their expenditures of campaign funds

File No. I-08-159
Page No. 3

for gifts, meals, and travel. These new requirements were effective July 1, 2008. To
ensure compliance with Commission regulations, you have requested further guidance
regarding the new regulation.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires state and local candidates and their controlled committees to file
periodic reports disclosing contributions received and expenditures made. Section 84211,
in pertinent part, requires a campaign statement to contain: (1) the total amount of
expenditures made during the period of the statement to persons who have received $100
or more (Section 84211(i)); (2) the total amount of expenditures made during the period
of the statement to persons who have received less than $100 (Section 84211(j)); and (3)
the name and street address for each person to whom an expenditure of $100 dollars or
more has been made during the period of the statement, including the amount of the
expenditure and a brief description of the consideration for which an expenditure was
made (Section 84211(k)).

Interpreting the "brief description" required for expenditures of $100 or more, the
Commission adopted Regulation 18421.7. This regulation, effective on July 1, 2008,
requires candidate controlled committees reporting an itemized expenditure for a gift, a
meal, or travel to "briefly describe the political, legislative, or governmental purpose of
the expenditure." Additionally, the regulation requires candidate controlled committees
to provide certain information for these types of expenditures, so that a person reviewing
the committee's campaign statement can assess the benefit to the candidate, members of
the candidate's household, or other individuals with the authority to expend the
committee's campaign funds. The specific information required for any particular
expenditure is determined by whether the expenditure is for a gift, a meal, or travel.

You have asked several specific questions regarding the application of Regulation
18421.7 to certain expenditures. We address each of your questions in turn.

1) When reporting expenditures for gifts, meals, and travel under new Regulation
18421.7, do the additional disclosure rules apply only to single transactions of $100 or
more, or do they apply to transactions that cumulatively reach $100 to a single vendor
during a reporting period?

By its express terms, Regulation 18421.7 applies to any "itemized expenditure
under Section 84211(k) or Section 84303." As stated above, Section 84211(k) requires
disclosing the name and address "[f]or each person to whom an expenditure of one
hundred dollars ($100) or more has been made during the period covered by the
campaign statement." In addition, Section 84303 imposes the disclosure required by
Section 84211(k) on expenditures of $500 or more made by an agent or independent
contractor on behalf of the candidate or committee, providing as follows:

File No. I-08-159
Page No. 4

"No expenditure of five hundred dollars ($500) or more shall be
made, other than overhead or normal operating expenses, by an agent or
independent contractor, including, but not limited to, an advertising
agency, on behalf of or for the benefit of any candidate or committee
unless it is reported by the candidate or committee as if the expenditure
were made directly by the candidate or committee."2

If multiple payments are made to the same person during a reporting period, you
ask whether the payments to the person must be cumulated in determining whether the
threshold for itemization has been met. This question is answered by Section 84211(k),
which provides that the term "expenditure," as used in the subdivision, "means any
individual payment or accrued expense, unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that a series of payments or accrued expenses are for a single service or
good." (Also see Willet Advice Letter, No. A-03-165.) Accordingly, when reporting an
expenditure for a meal, a gift, or travel under Regulation 18421.7, the additional
disclosure rules apply only to single payments unless a series of payments is for a single
service or good.

For example, if a candidate uses campaign funds for a three-night stay at a hotel at
a rate of $75 per night, the candidate cannot avoid itemizing payments to the hotel, under
Section 84211(k), by making daily payments of $75 in lieu of a single $225 payment.
Because the payments are for a single service, the daily payments must be cumulated in
determining whether the itemization threshold has been met.

2) Do the additional disclosure rules for travel under Regulation 18421.7 apply
to gas or transportation within a candidate's jurisdiction, a legislator's travel between
his or her district and Sacramento, or travel within California by a statewide candidate?

Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(3) impose additional disclosure requirements on
expenditures of campaign funds for travel. While an expenditure for air transportation is
clearly an expenditure for travel regardless of the ultimate destination, other
transportation expenditures are harder to classify because some basic ground
transportation expenses must be distinguished from travel expenditures. In the absence
of specific factual circumstances, we offer the following guidelines for distinguishing
basic ground transportation expenses, including the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, or
public transit fare, from travel expenditures requiring the disclosure of specific
information under Regulation 18421.7.

2 When reporting an expenditure by the candidate or committee to an agent or independent
contractor, the payment is not subject to additional disclosure under Regulation [1842] 7. However, the
disclosure requirements of Regulation 18421.7 do apply to an expenditure of $500 of more by the agent or
independent contractor for a gift, a meal, or travel reported under Section 84303. Moreover, a candidate or
committee may not route an expenditure for a gift, a meal, or travel through an agent or independent
contractor in an attempt to avoid additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7.

File No. 1-08-159
Page No. 5

Local Officials and Candidates

Expenditures of campaign funds for the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, public
transit fare, or other ground transportation costs for transportation in, or within a 50-mile
radius of, the official's or candidate's jurisdiction are not expenditures for travel subject
to the additional disclosure of Regulation 18421.7(a) or (a)(3).

State Officials or Candidates, Elected by District

Expenditures of campaign funds for the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, or public
transit fare, or other ground transportation costs for transportation in, or within a 50-mile
radius of, the official's or candidate's district or the City of Sacramento are not
expenditures for travel subject to the additional disclosure of Regulation 18421.7(a) or
(a)(3). However, expenditures for ground transportation from the official's or
candidate's district to the City of Sacramento are expenditures for travel subject to
additional disclosure, unless the transportation costs are for a daily commute.

State Officials or Candidates, Elected by Statewide Vote

Expenditures of campaign funds for the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, or public
transit fare, or other ground transportation costs for transportation in, or within a 50-mile
radius, of the official's or candidate's city of domicile, the City of Sacramento, or a city
in which the official or candidate has an established office are not expenditures for travel
subject to the additional disclosure of Regulation 18421.7(a) or (a)(3). However,
expenditures for ground transportation between any of these cities are expenditures for
travel subject to additional disclosure, unless the transportation costs are for a daily
commute.

3) Do the additional disclosure rules for meals under Regulation 18421.7 apply
to food provided to precinct walkers, a single expenditure of $100 or more to purchase
both office supplies and food, catered food served at an official's fundraiser, or a room
service order if the total charges to the hotel require itemization?

To answer your questions regarding the appropriate disclosure for various
purchases of food and beverages, we must first determine what constitutes a meal for
purposes of Regulation 18421.7. Evidencing the Commission's intent in selecting the
term "meal," the recording of the discussion of the regulation at the February 14, 2008,
Commission Meeting reveals that the term "meal" was explained as food and beverages
suitable for a typical breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Accordingly, merely providing drinks,
snacks, or appetizers would not require additional reporting as an expenditure of
campaign funds for a meal but may require additional disclosure as an expenditure of
campaign funds for a gift.

File No. 1-08-159
Page No. 6


Food and Beverages Provided to Precinct Walkers

The question of whether a payment for food and beverages provided to campaign workers, either paid employees or volunteer staff, is an expenditure for a meal or a gift presents a unique question of interpretation. In most instances, a payment for minimal food and beverages provided to campaign workers would not be considered an expenditure for a gift requiring additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(1) because the food and beverages are provided in return for the workers' services on behalf of the campaign. In addition, we do not believe the Commission meant to classify payments for minimal food and beverages, such as boxed lunches for precinct walkers, pizza for a staff party, or a hamburger and fries provided to workers while performing their campaign duties, as the types of payments requiring additional disclosure as expenditures for a meal under Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(2).

Accordingly, a payment for minimal food and beverages provided to campaign staff does not require additional disclosure as an expenditure for a gift or a meal under Regulation 18421.7. Note, however, that this conclusion is limited to food and beverages of minimal value provided to staff while engaged in campaign activities. A payment for a bottle of wine provided to a campaign worker would constitute an expenditure of campaign funds for a gift subject to additional disclosure. Likewise, a payment to take a campaign worker to a restaurant for a dinner of more than minimal value would be an expenditure of campaign funds for a meal subject to additional disclosure.

Single Expenditure for Office Supplies and Food and Beverages

If making a single expenditure of campaign funds of $100 or more for multiple items, the campaign statement reflecting the expenditure must provide a brief description of the items. Where the items are unrelated, the brief description provided must describe both items sufficiently. (Section 84211.) In describing a single expenditure for office supplies and food and beverages, the code "OFC" is a sufficient description for the office supplies as this is a predetermined expenditure classification provided on Commission Form 460 for office expenses such as rent, utilities, office, equipment, furniture, and other office supplies. However, the committee must also provide a brief description of the food and beverages for which the payment was made.

Because there is no predetermined expenditure classification provided for food and beverages (except the codes "TRC" and "TRS" that only encompass meals purchased while traveling), the food and beverages must be briefly described. If the payment for the food and beverages has been made to provide a disclosable meal, as described above, the committee must provide a brief description that includes all of the additional information for an expenditure for a meal required by Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(2). Moreover, if the payment for the food and beverages has not been made to provide a meal, the payment may still need to be reported as an expenditure for a gift under Regulation

File No. I-08-159
Page No. 7

18421.7(a) and (a)(1) if provided to a recipient who has not provided equal or greater
consideration.³

Catered Food and Beverages at an Official's Fundraiser

To determine whether a payment for catered food and beverages at a fundraising
event is subject to additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7, we must first
distinguish between an expenditure of campaign funds by a hosting candidate for food
and beverages provided at a fundraising event for the candidate's own committee and an
expenditure of campaign funds for tickets to another candidate's or committee's
fundraising event at which food and beverages will be provided.

For an expenditure of campaign funds made by the hosting candidate for catered
food and beverages served at the candidate's own fundraising event, the expenditure must
be reported under Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(2) as an expenditure for a meal if the
food and beverages constitute a meal as described above. However, if the candidate only
provides hors d'oeuvres and drinks, these items are not considered a meal, and the
candidate is not required to provide the additional disclosure for an expenditure for a
meal. Additionally, an expenditure for hors d'oeuvres and drinks served by a candidate
at his or her own fundraiser is not considered a gift to the attendees as these items are
provided only because the attendees have made a contribution by purchasing tickets for
the fundraiser. Accordingly, a candidate's expenditure for hors d'oeuvres and drinks
provided at the candidate's own fundraiser to paying attendees is not an expenditure for a
gift subject to additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(1).⁴

For an expenditure of campaign funds for tickets to a fundraising event of another
candidate or committee at which catered food and beverages will be provided, the entire
purchase price of a ticket to the fundraising event is considered a contribution to the
hosting candidate or committee under Section 82015(c). In light of Section 82015, an
expenditure for tickets to another candidate's or committee's fundraising event is more
accurately disclosed as a contribution to the hosting candidate or committee even if food
and beverages are provided at the event. Accordingly, a candidate reporting an

³ Note that a payment for minimal food and beverages provided to committee staff is not
considered an expenditure for a gift or a meal subject to additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7. In
the examples you have provided, neither an expenditure for office supplies, chips, and sodas, nor an
expenditure for office supplies, hamburgers, chips, and sodas, is an expenditure subject to additional
disclosure under Regulation 18421.7 so long as the food and beverages are provided exclusively to
committee staff.

⁴ While we find that a payment of campaign funds for hors d'oeuvres and drinks served at a
fundraising event does not constitute a gift to those attending the fundraiser subject to additional disclosure
as an expenditure for a gift under Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(1), the entire purchase price for the tickets
to the fundraising event is considered a contribution to the candidate under Section 82015(c). In other
words, the value of the food and beverages provided by the hosting candidate at a fundraising event cannot
be subtracted from the ticket price in determining the amount of the contribution to the hosting candidate.

File No. I-08-159
Page No. 8

expenditure of campaign funds for tickets to a fundraising event of another candidate or
committee should disclose the expenditure as a contribution, and the expenditure is not
considered an expenditure for a gift, a meal, or travel subject to additional disclosure
under Regulation 18421.7.

Room Service Orders

In addition to a brief description of the political, legislative, or governmental
purpose of an expenditure for travel, Regulation 18421.7(a)(3) requires the disclosure of
the "date or dates of travel, the destination, the goods or services paid for by the
expenditure, the number of individuals for whom the expenditure was paid, and whether
these individuals included the candidate, a member of the candidate's household ..., or an
individual with authority to approve expenditures of the committee's campaign funds."
An expenditure of $100 or more made for either a hotel stay or for room service
purchased while traveling must be reported as an expenditure for travel under Regulation
18421.7(a) and (a)(3).

If a single payment of $100 or more is made for both the costs of the hotel stay
and the room service ordered while traveling, the single payment must be reported as an
expenditure for travel. Because Regulation 18421.7(a)(3) requires that the goods or
services paid for by the expenditure must be disclosed, the fact that the payment was
made for both the hotel stay and for room service must be disclosed when reporting the
expenditure.

4) What is the definition of "gift" as used in Regulation 18421.7, and do the
exceptions for the term gift found in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or other
Commission regulations apply?

Section 82028(a) of the Act defines a "gift" and provides the following:

"'Gift' means, except as provided in subdivision (b), any payment
that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the
public without regard to official status."

Generally, a gift of campaign funds for purposes of Regulation 18421.7 is any
payment of campaign funds that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received.⁵ Notwithstanding the fact that

⁵ While a payment of campaign funds for a meal or travel may be a gift as defined, Regulation
18421.7(a)(1) does not impose additional disclosure requirements on an expenditure reportable as an
expenditure for a meal or travel. Despite the fact that an expenditure for a meal or travel may also be a gift
to the recipient, an expenditure for a meal, other than a meal reported as an expenditure for travel, must be

File No. I-08-159
Page No. 9

the general definition of the term "gift" found in Section 82028(a) is applicable to the gift
of campaign funds under Regulation 18421.7, we must separately determine whether the
exceptions to the term "gift" provided in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or other
Commission regulations apply.

Section 82028(b) provides exceptions from the gift rules for informational
materials, gifts returned to the donor or delivered to a nonprofit entity, gifts from family
members, campaign contributions, a devise or inheritance, and certain plaques and
trophies. On their face, the exceptions for gifts from a family member and for a devise or
inheritance are simply inapplicable to a gift of campaign funds. Additionally, the
exception for gifts returned to the donor or delivered to a nonprofit cannot be reasonably
applied to a gift of campaign funds as it would allow a candidate to avoid disclosure
under Regulation 18421.7 based upon the actions of the recipient despite the candidate's
intentions.

Moreover, the exceptions found in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, and other
Commission regulations (see, e.g. Regulation 18950.1(c)) serve the purpose of excluding
certain items given to public officials from the Act's gift reporting requirements and gift
limits. This purpose is wholly unrelated to the reporting of campaign expenditures
pursuant to Section 84211. Accordingly, we find no reason to extend the exceptions
found in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or other Commission regulations to the
disclosure of gifts of campaign funds as required by Regulation 18421.7.

5) If an itemized expenditure for a gift, requiring additional disclosure under
Regulation 18421.7, is made for a large group of recipients, may the committee use a
term or phrase to describe the recipients?

Under Regulation 18421.7(a)(1), if an itemized expenditure is made for a gift to a
group of recipients, the name of each recipient who received a benefit of $50 or more
must be disclosed. (Regulation 18421.7(a)(1).) A committee may use a term or phrase to
describe the recipients only when the benefit to each recipient is less than $50 or if the
name of each recipient who received a benefit of $50 or more is also disclosed.⁶

reported only under Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(2), and an expenditure for travel must be reported only
under Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(3).

⁶ We note that there are additional recordkeeping requirements for itemized expenditures by
candidate controlled committees found in Regulation 18401(a)(5). Under this regulation, the names of
each individual for whom an itemized expenditure for a meal or travel was paid must be maintained in the
committee's records, even if the expenditure was made for a large group of recipients, regardless of the
value of the benefit to each recipient.

File No. I-08-159
Page No. 10


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

18421.7, the additional disclosure rules apply only to single payments of $100 or more,
or $500 or more if made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the candidate
or committee, unless a series of payments is for a single service or good.
2. As discussed below, an expenditure of campaign funds for airfare is always an

sought by the candidate.
3. As discussed below, the determination of whether an expenditure for food and
beverages is subject to the additional disclosure rules for an expenditure for a meal under
Regulation 18421.7 is based on whether the food or beverages constitute a meal to the
recipient. Food and beverages not constituting a meal may, however, be subject to

recipient has provided equal or greater consideration.
4. Generally, a gift of campaign funds for purposes of Regulation 18421.7 is any
payment of campaign funds that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. We find no reason to extend
the exceptions to the term "gift" contained in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or any
of the other exceptions to gifts set forth in Commission regulations, to the disclosure of

5. A committee may use a term or phrase to describe the recipients of a gift under

sections.facts:
Under new Regulation 18421.7, state and local candidates and their controlled
committees must provide detailed descriptions of their expenditures of campaign ftrnds
for gifts, meals, and travel. These new requirements were effective July 1, 2008. To
ensure compliance with Commission regulations, you have requested further guidance
regarding the new regulation.
sections.analysis:
The Act requires state and local candidates and their controlled committees to file
periodic reports disclosing contributions received and expenditures made. Section 84211,
in pertinent part, requires a campaign statement to contain: (l) the total amount of
expenditures made during the period of the statement to persons who have received $100
or more (Section 8a211(i)); (2) the total amount of expenditures made during the period
of the statement to persons who have received less than $100 (Section84211()); and (3)
the name and street address for each person to whom an expenditure of $100 dollars or
more has been made during the period of the statement, including the amount of the
expenditure and a brief description of the consideration for which an expenditure was
made (Section 8421 1(k)).
Interpreting the "brief description" required for expenditures of $100 or more, the
Commission adopted Regulation 18421.7. This regulation, effective on July 1, 2008,
requires candidate controlled committees reporting an itemized expenditure for a gift, a
meal, or travel to "briefly describe the political, legislative, or govemmental purpose of
the expenditure." Additionally, the regulation requires candidate controlled committees
to provide certain information for these types of expenditures, so that a person reviewing
the committee's campaign statement can assess the benefit to the candidate, members of
the candidate's household, or other individuals with the authority to expend the
committee's campaign funds. The specific information required for any particular
expenditure is determined by whether the expenditure is for a gift, a meal, or travel.
You have asked several specific questions regarding the application of Regulation
I842I.7 to certain expenditures. We address each of your questions in tum.
I ) When reporting expenditures for gtfts, meals, and travel under new Regulation
18421.7, do the additional disclosure rules apply only to single transactions of $100 or
more, or do they apply to transactions that cumulatively reach $100 to a single vendor
during a reporting period?
By its express terms, Regulation 18421.7 applies to any "itemized expenditure
under Section 84211(k) or Section 84303." As stated above, Section 84211(k) requires
disclosing the name and address "[f]or each person to whom an expenditure of one
hundred dollars ($tOO; or more has been made during the period covered by the
campaign statement." In addition, Section 84303 imposes the disclosure required by
Section 84211(k) on expenditures of $500 or more made by an agent or independent
contractor on behalf of the candidate or committee, providing as follows:
"No expenditure of five hundred dollars ($5OO; or more shall be
made, other than overhead or normal operating expenses, by an agent or
independent contractor, including, but not limited to, an advertising
agency, on behalf of or for the benefit of any candidate or committee
unless it is reported by the candidate or committee as if the expenditure
were made directly by the candidate or committee."2
If multiple payments are made to the same person during a reporting period, you
ask whether the payments to the person must be cumulated in determining whether the
threshold for itemization has been met. This question is answered by Section 842II(k),
which provides that the term "expenditure," as used in the subdivision, "means any
individual payment or accmed expense, unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that a series of payments or accrued expenses are for a single service or
good." (Also see Willet Advice Letter, No. A-03-165.) Accordingly, when reporting an
expenditure for a meal, a gift, or travel under Regulation 18421.7, the additional
disclosure rules apply only to single payments unless a series of payments is for a single
service or good.
For example, if a candidate uses campaign funds for a three-night stay at a hotel at
a rate of $75 per night, the candidate cannot avoid itemizing payments to the hotel, under
Section 84211(k), by making daily payments of $75 in lieu of a single $225 payment.
Because the payments are for a single service, the daily payments must be cumulated in
determining whether the itemization threshold has been met.
2) Do the additional disclosure rulesfor travel under Regttlatton 18421.7 apply
to gas or transportation within a candidate's jurisdictton, a legislator's travel between
his or her district and Sacramento, or travel within California by a statewide candidate?
Regulation 18421.7(a) and (a)(3) impose additional disclosure requirements on
expenditures of campaign funds for travel. While an expenditure for air transportation is
clearly an expenditure for travel regardless of the ultimate destination, other
transportation expenditures are harder to classify because some basic ground
transportation expenses must be distinguished from travel expenditures. In the absence
of specific factual circumstances, we offer the following guidelines for distinguishing
basic ground transportation expenses, including the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, or
public transit fare, from travel expenditures requiring the disclosure of specific
information under Regulation I842I.7.
' When reporting an expenditure by the candidate or committee to an agent or independent
contractor, the payment is not subject to additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7. However, the
disclosure requirements of Regulation 18421 .7 do apply to an expenditure of $500 of more by the agent or
independent contractor for a giti, a meal, or travel reported under Section 84303. Moreover. a candidate or
committee may not route an expenditure for a gift, a meal, or travel through an agent or independent
contrastor in an attempt to avoid additional disclosure under Regulation 1842 1.7.
Local Officials and Candidates
Expenditures of campaign funds for the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, public
transit fare, or other ground transportation costs for transportation in, or within a SO-mile
radius of, the official's or candidate's jurisdiction are not expenditures for travel subject
to the additional disclosure of Regulation 18421.7(a) or (aX3).
State Officials or Candidates. Elected by District
Expenditures of campaign funds for the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, or public
transit fare, or other ground transportation costs for transportation in, or within a 5O-mile
radius of, the official's or candidate's district or the City of Sacramento are not
expenditures for travel subject to the additional disclosure of Regulation 1842I.7(a) or
(aX3). However, expenditures for ground transportation from the official's or
candidate's district to the City of Sacramento are expenditures for travel subject to
additional disclosure; unless the transportation costs are for a daily commute.
State Officials or Candidates. Elected by Statewide Vote
Expenditures of campaign funds for the purchase of gasoline, cab fare, or public
transit fare, or other ground transportation costs for transportation in, or within a 50-mile
radius, of the official's or candidate's city of domicile, the City of Sacramento, or a city
in which the official or candidate has an established office are not expenditures for travel
subject to the additional disclosure of Regulation 18421.7(a) or (aX3). However,
expenditures for ground transportation between any of these cities are expenditures for
travel subject to additional disclosure, unless the transportation costs are for a daily
commute.
3) Do the additional disclosure rules for meals under Regulation 18421.7 apply
to food provided to precinct walkers, a single expenditure of $100 or more to purchase
both ffice supplies andfood, cateredfood served at an fficial'sfundraiser, or a room
service order if the total charges to the hotel require itemization?
To answer your questions regarding the appropriate disclosure for various
purchases of food and beverages, we must first determine what constitutes a meal for
purposes of Regulation 18421.7. Evidencing the Commission's intent in selecting the
term "meal," the recording of the discussion of the regulation at the February 14,2008,
Commission Meeting reveals that the term "meal" was explained as food and beverages
suitable for a typical breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Accordingly, merely providing drinks,
snacks, or appetizers would not require additional reporting as an expenditure of
campaign funds for a meal but may require additional disclosure as an expenditure of
campaign funds for a gift.
Food and Beverages Provided to Precinct Walkers
The question of whether a payment for food and beverages provided to campaign
workers, either paid employees or volunteer staff, is an expenditure for a meal or a gift
presents a unique question of interpretation. In most instances, a payment for minimal
food and beverages provided to campaign workers would not be considered an
expenditure for a gift requiring additional disclosure under Regulation 1842L.7(a) and
(aX1) because the food and beverages are provided in retum for the workers' services on
behalf of the campaign. In addition, we do not believe the Commission meant to classify
payments for minimal food and beverages, such as boxed lunches for precinct walkers,
pizzafor a staff pafty, or a hamburger and fries provided to workers while performing
their campaign duties, as the types of payments requiring additional disclosure as
expenditures for a meal under Regulation fia21.7(a) and (a)(2).
Accordingly, a payment for minimal food and beverages provided to campaign
staff does not require additional disclosure as an expenditure for a gift or a meal under
Regulation 18421.7. Note, however, that this conclusion is limited to food and beverages
of minimal value provided to staff while engaged in campaign activities. A payment for
a bottle of wine provided to a campaign worker would constitute an expenditure of
campaign funds for a gift subject to additional disclosure. Likewise, a payment to take a
campaign worker to a restaurant for a dinner of more than minimal value would be an
expenditure of campaign funds for a meal subject to additional disclosure.
Single Expenditure for Office Supplies and Food and Beverages
If making a single expenditure of campaign funds of $100 or more for multiple
items, the campaign statement reflecting the expenditure must provide a brief description
of the items. Where the items are unrelated, the brief description provided must describe
both items sufficiently. (Section 84211.) In describing a single expenditure for office
supplies and food and beverages, the code "OFC" is a sufficient description for the office
supplies as this is a predetermined expenditure classification provided on Commission
Form 460 for office expenses such as rent, utilities, office, equipment, furniture, and
other office supplies. However, the committee must also provide a brief description of
the food and beverages for which the payment was made.
Because there is no predetermined expenditure classification provided for food
and beverages (except the codes "TRC" and "TRS" that only encompass meals purchased
while traveling), the food and beverages must be briefly described. If the payment for the
food and beverages has been made to provide a disclosable meal, as described above, the
committee must provide a brief description that includes all of the additional information
for an expenditure for a meal required by Regulation 18421.7(a) and (aX2). Moreover, if
the payment for the food and beverages has not been made to provide a meal, the
payment may still need to be reported as an expenditure for a gift under Regulation
1842I.7(a) and (a)(l) if provided to a recipient who has not provided equal or greater
consideration.l
Catered Food and Beverages at an Official's Fundraiser
To determine whether a payment for catered food and beverages at a fundraising
event is subject to additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7,wi must first
distinguish between an expenditure of campaign funds by a hosting candidate for food
and beverages provided at a fundraising event for the candidate's own committee and an
expenditure of campaign ftrnds for tickets to another candidate's or committee's
fundraising event at which food and beverages will be provided.
For an expenditure of campaign funds made by the hosting candidate for catered
food and beverages served at the candidate's own fundraising event, the expenditure must
be reported under Regulation 18421 .7 (a) and (a)(2) as an expenditure for a meal if the
food and beverages constitute a meal as described above. However, if the candidate only
provides hors d'oeuvres and drinks, these items are not considered a meal, and the
candidate is not required to provide the additional disclosure for an expenditure for a
meal. Additionally, an expenditure for hors d'oeuvres and drinks served by a candidate
at his or her own fundraiser is not considered a gift to the attendees as these items are
provided only because the attendees have made a contribution by purchasing tickets for
the fundraiser. Accordingly, a candidate's expenditure for hors d'oeuvres and drinks
provided at the candidate's own fundraiser to paying attendees is not an expenditure for a
gift subject to additional disclosure under Regulation l84zl.7(a) and (a)(l).a
For an expenditure of campaign funds for tickets to a fundraising event of another
candidate or committee at which catered food and beverages will be provided, the entire
purchase price of a ticket to the fundraising event is considered a contribution to the
hosting candidate or committee under Section 82015(c). In light of Section 82015, an
expenditure for tickets to another candidate's or committee's fundraising event is more
accurately disclosed as a contribution to the hosting candidate or committee even if food
and beverages are provided at the event. Accordingly, a candidate reporting an
I Note that a payment for minimal food and beverages provided to committee staff is not
considered an expenditure for a gift or a meal subject to additional disclosure under Regulation l g42 1.7. In
the examples you have provided, neither an expenditure for office supplies, chips, and sodas, nor an
expenditure for office supplies, hamburgers, chips, and sodas, is an expenditure subject to additional
disclosure under Regulation 1842 1.7 so long as the food and beverages are provided exclusively to
committee staff.
' while we find that a payment of campaign ftnds for hors d'oeuvres and drinks served at a
fundraising event does not constitute a gift to those attending the tundraiser subject to additional disclosure
asanexpendituretbragiftunderRegulation lB42l .j{a)and(aXI),theentirepurchasepricefbrthetickets
tothefundraisingeventisconsideredacontributiontothecandidateunderSectionS20i5(c). Inother
words' the value of the fbod and beverages provided by the hosting candidate at a fundraising event cannot
be subtracted tiom the ticket price in determining the amount of the contribution to the hosting candidate.
expenditure of campaign funds for tickets to a fundraising event of another candidate or
committee should disclose the expenditure as a contribution, and the expenditure is not
considered an expenditure for a gift, a meal, or travel subject to additional disclosure
under Regulation 18421.7 .
Room Service Orders
In addition to a brief description of the political, legislative, or governmental
purpose of an expenditure for travel, Regulation 18421.7(a)(3) requires the disclosure of
the "date or dates of travel, the destination, the goods or services paid for by the
expenditure, the number of individuals for whom the expenditure was paid, and whether
these individuals included the candidate, a member of the candidate's household ..., or an
individual with authority to approve expenditures of the committee's campaign funds."
An expenditure of $100 or more made for either a hotel stay or for room service
purchased while traveling must be reported as an expenditure for travel under Regulation
18421.7(a) and (a)(3).
If a single payment of $100 or more is made for both the costs of the hotel stay
and the room service ordered while traveling, the single payment must be reported as an
expenditure for travel. Because Regulation 18421.7(a)(3) requires that the goods or
services paid for by the expenditure must be disclosed, the fact that the payment was
made for both the hotel stay and for room service must be disclosed when reporting the
expenditure.
4) what is the definition of "gtft" as used in Regulation rg42r.7, and do the
exceptions for the term gift found in Section 82028(b), Regulatton 18942, or other
Commission re gulations apply ?
Section 82028(a) of the Act defines a "gift" and provides the following:
"'Gift' means, except as provided in subdivision (b), any payment
that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration ofequal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the
public without regard to official status."
Generally, a gift of campaign funds for purposes of Regulation 18421 .7 is any
payment of campaign funds that confers a personal benefit_on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received.s Notwithstanding the fact that
5 While a payment of campaign funds for a meal or travel may be a gifl as defined. Regulation
18421 .7(aX l) does not impose additional disclosure requirements on an expenditure reportable as an
expenditure for a meal or travel. Despite the tact that an expenditure tbr a meal or travel may also be a gift
to the recipient, an expenditure fbr a meal, other than a meal reported as an expenditure tbr tiavel, must be
the general definition of the term "gift" found in Section 82028(a) is applicable to the gift
of campaign funds under Regulation 18421.'7 , we must separately deterrnine whether the
exceptions to the term "gift" provided in Section 82028(bi, Regulation lg941,or other
Commission regulations apply.
Section 82028(b) provides exceptions from the gift mles for informational
materials, gifts retumed to the donor or delivered to a nonprofit entity, gifts from family
members, campaign contributions, a devise or inheritance, and ""rtoi, flaques and
trophies. on their face, the exceptions for gifts from a family member and fo, u devise or
inheritance are simply inapplicable to a gift of campaign funis. Additionally, the
exception for gifts retumed to the donor or delivered to a nonprofit cannot be reasonably
applied to a gift of campaign funds as it would allow a candidate to avoid disclosure
under Regulation 18421.7 based upon the actions of the recipient despite the candidate,s
intentions.
Moreover, the exceptions found in Section 82028(b), Regulation lgg41,and other
Commission regulations(see, e.g. Regulation 18950.1(c)) serveih" p.,.pore of excluding
certain items given to public officials from the Act's gift reporting r"qui."."rts and gift
limits. This purpose is wholly unrelated to the reporting of campaignLxpenditures
pursuant to Section y!?IL Accordingly, we find no reason to "it"nd 
the exceptions
found in Section 82028(b), Regulation l8g42,or other Commission regulations to the
disclosure of gifts of campaign funds as required by Regulation 1g421.7.
5 ) If an itemized expenditure for a gtft, requiring additioneil disclosure under
Regulation 18421.7, is madefor a large group ofiecipiints, may the committee ttse a
term or phrase to describe the reciptents?
Under Regulation 18421.7(a)(1), if an itemized expenditure is made for a gift to a
group of recipients, the name of each recipient who receivid a benefit of $50 or more
must be disclosed- (Regulation 1842I.7(a)(1).) A committee may use a term or phrase to
describe the recipients only when the benefit to each recipient is llss than $50 or if the
name of each recipient who received a benefit of $50 or more is also disclosed.6

under Regul
y under Regulation 18421 .7(a) and (aX2), and an expenditure for travel must be reported only

6 we note that there are additional recordkeeping requirements for itemized expenditures by
candidate controlled committees tound in Regulation iS+0lf ojtSl. Under this regulation. the names of
errc'lr individual for whom an itemized expenditure for a meal or travel was paid must be maintained in the
committee's rectlrds. even if the expenditure was made for a large group of recipients. regarJte.ss otttre

lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (gL6) 322-5660.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: l. When reporting expenditures of campaign funds for gifts, meals, and travel
under new Regulation 18421 .7, do the additional disclosure rules apply only to sirlgle
transactions of $100 or more, or do they apply to transactions that cumulatively reach
$100 to a single vendor during a reporting period?
2. Do the additional disclosure rules under Regulation 18421.7 for an expenditure
for travel apply to gas or transportation within a candidate's jurisdiction, a legislator's
travel between his or her district and Sacramento, or travel within California by a
statewide candidate?
3. Do the additional disclosure rules under Regulation 18421.7 for an expenditure
for a meal apply to food provided to precinct walkers, a single expenditure of $100 or
more to purchase both office supplies and food. catered food served at an official's
fundraiser, or a room service order when traveling if the total charges to the hotel require
itemization?
4. What is the definition of "gift" as used in Regulation 18421.7, and do the
exceptions for the term found in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or other
Commission regulations apply?
5. [f an itemized expenditure for a gift is made for a large group of recipients,
may the committee use a term or phrase to describe the recipients?

CONCLUSION: 1. When reporting expenditures for meals, gifts, or travel under Regulation
18421.7, the additional disclosure rules apply only to single payments of $100 or more,
or $500 or more if made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the candidate
or committee, unless a series of payments is for a single service or good.
2. As discussed below, an expenditure of campaign funds for airfare is always an
expenditure for "travel" subject to the additional disclosure requirements of Regulation
18421.7. However, payments for the costs of ground transportation within a certain area
of the candidate's residence or jurisdiction may not be subject to the additional disclosure
requirements of Regul ation 18421.7 depending, as discussed below, on the office held or
sought by the candidate.
3. As discussed below, the determination of whether an expenditure for food and
beverages is subject to the additional disclosure rules for an expenditure for a meal under
Regulation 18421.7 is based on whether the food or beverages constitute a meal to the
recipient. Food and beverages not constituting a meal may, however, be subject to
additional disclosure under Regulation 18421.7 as an expenditure for a gift unless the
recipient has provided equal or greater consideration.
4. Generally, a gift of campaign funds for purposes of Regulation 18421.7 is any
payment of campaign funds that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. We find no reason to extend
the exceptions to the term "gift" contained in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or any
of the other exceptions to gifts set forth in Commission regulations, to the disclosure of
gifts of campaign funds as required by Regulation 18421.7 .
5. A committee may use a term or phrase to describe the recipients of a gift under
Regulation 18421.7 only when the benefit to each recipient is less than $50 or only if the
name of each recipient who received a benefit of $50 or more is also disclosed.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 November 13, 2008 Richard Rios Olsen Hagel & Fishburn LLP 555 Capital Mall, Suite 1425 Sacramento, CA 95814-4602 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-08-159 Dear Mr. Rios: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you seek general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) QUESTIONS 1. When reporting expenditures of campaign funds for gifts, meals, and travel under new Regulation 18421.7, do the additional disclosure rules apply only to single transactions of $100 or more, or do they apply to transactions that cumulatively reach $100 to a single vendor during a reporting period? 2. Do the additional disclosure rules under Regulation 18421.7 for an expenditure for travel apply to gas or transportation within a candidate's jurisdiction, a legislator's travel between his or her district and Sacramento, or travel within California by a statewide candidate? 3. Do the additional disclosure rules under Regulation 18421.7 for an expenditure for a meal apply to food provided to precinct walkers, a single expenditure of $100 or more to purchase both office supplies and food, catered food served at an official's fundraiser, or a room service order when traveling if the total charges to the hotel require itemization? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. I-08-159 Page No. 2 4. What is the definition of "gift" as used in Regulation 18421.7, and do the exceptions for the term found in Section 82028(b), Regulation 18942, or other Commission regulations apply? 5. If an itemized expenditure for a gift is made for a large group of recipients, may the committee use a term or phrase to describe the recipients? CONCLUSIONS 1. When reporting expenditures for meals, gifts, or travel under Regulation 18421.7, the additional disclosure rules apply only to single payments of $100 or more, or $500 or more if made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the candidate or committee, unless a series of payments is for a single service or good. 2. As discussed below, an expenditure of campaign funds for airfare is always an expenditure for "travel" subject to the additional disclosure requirements of Regulation 18421.7. However, payments for the costs of ground transportation within a certain area of the candidate's residence or jurisdiction may not be subject to the additional disclosure requirements of
